excerpts: Across the ecosystem: There are now more than 10,000 active public MCP servers, covering everything from developer tools to Fortune 500 deployments;

Claude now has a directory with over 75 [connectors](https://claude.com/connectors) (powered by MCP), and we recently launched [Tool Search and Programmatic Tool Calling](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/advanced-tool-use) capabilities in our API to help optimize production-scale MCP deployments, handling thousands of tools efficiently and reducing latency in complex agent workflows.

MCP now has an official, community-driven [Registry](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/registry) for discovering available MCP servers, and the [November 25th](https://blog.modelcontextprotocol.io/posts/2025-11-25-first-mcp-anniversary/) spec release introduced many new features, including asynchronous operations, statelessness, server identity, and official extensions.

Dec 9, 2025
Today, we’re donating the [Model Context Protocol](https://modelcontextprotocol.io) (MCP) to the Agentic AI Foundation (AAIF), a directed fund under the [Linux Foundation](https://www.linuxfoundation.org/) , co-founded by Anthropic, Block and OpenAI, with support from Google, Microsoft, Amazon Web Services (AWS), Cloudflare, and Bloomberg.

excerpts: September 8, 2025 · David Soria Parra (Lead Maintainer), Adam Jones (Registry Maintainer), Tadas Antanavicius (Registry Maintainer), Toby Padilla (Registry Maintainer), Theodora Chu (MCP PM at Anthropic)
Today, we’re launching the Model Context Protocol (MCP) Registry—an open catalog and API for publicly available MCP servers to improve discoverability and implementation.
Our goal is to standardize how servers are distributed and discovered, providing a primary source of truth that sub-registries can build upon.
The MCP Registry is now available in preview.
By standardizing how servers are distributed and discovered, we’re expanding their reach while making it easier for clients to get connected.

excerpts: nthropic’s Model Context Protocol (MCP), first released in November 2024, has become the universal standard for connecting agents to tools and data — often described as the “USB-C for AI.
The November 2025 spec revision added task tracking, simplified OAuth flows, and a formal extension mechanism.

It uses JSON-RPC 2.0 over HTTP with OAuth 2.1 authentication and has achieved remarkable adoption: more than 10,000 published MCP servers, support from every major platform including Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, Copilot, VS Code, and Cursor, plus a dedicated registry with approximately 2,000 entries.
Google’s Agent-to-Agent (A2A) Protocol, launched in April 2025 and updated to v0.3 in July 2025, addresses the complementary challenge of agent-to-agent collaboration.
Over 150 organizations now support A2A, including major enterprise software vendors like Salesforce, SAP, ServiceNow, and Adobe.
citations.title: The Agentic AI Infrastructure Landscape in 2025 — 2026: A Strategic Analysis for Tool-Builders | by Sri Srujan Mandava | Feb, 2026 | Medium

excerpts: Our recently launched Agent2Agent (A2A) protocol is getting a lot of attention, and for good reason. This open interoperability protocol is designed to make it easy for AI agents to communicate with one another, no matter its foundation. It’s also a powerful complement to Anthropic’s Model Context Protocol (MCP).
e/overview) is a fully managed runtime in Vertex AI that helps you deploy your custom agents to production with built-in testing, release, and reliability at a global, secure scale.
The protocol can be used as an extension of the Agent2Agent (A2A) protocol and Model Context Protocol (MCP).

References to the November 2025 spec, the MCP Registry, and enterprise-focused discussions collectively substantiate the field value that governance, OAuth 2.1-based authorization, CIMD-like identity metadata, and gateway-based scaling are central to the MCP evolution. excerpts: Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open protocol that enables seamless integration between LLM applications and external data sources and tools.

excerpts:
The [June 2025 specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/changelog) established the first stable model of MCP for connecting agents to external tools and data sources. It defined how a server could expose capabilities, how clients could discover resources and prompts, and how structured tool invocation worked. It enabled early interoperability in agent ecosystems without requiring every integration to be a custom API.
However, the June version had notable limitations that restricted its use in production systems:
Execution was primarily synchronous. Long-running or complex work was not supported.
Authorization was basic, with a coarse trust model and limited granularity.
Client registration and identity handling were under-specified and inconsistent.
Governance and lifecycle controls were minimal.

excerpts: > How to reduce tool context consumption by 94% using Google’s Agent Development Kit (ADK) combined with a custom tool search.
The “Tool Search” pattern empowers AI agents to effectively manage hundreds or even thousands of tools by dynamically discovering and loading them strictly on demand.

**Discovery
** The agent has access to a lightweight search tool. It queries a local index (using BM25) to find relevant tools based on names and docstrings

**Loading**
Once the agent identifies a useful tool, it calls a load tool. The system intercepts this call and dynamically injects the full tool definition into the agent's context for the next turn.

article demonstrates how to implement this robust, scalable pattern in **Google’s Agent Development Kit (ADK)** , enabling your agents to access thousands of tools (including MCP tools) with negligible latency

The [June 2025 specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/changelog) established the first stable model of MCP for connecting agents to external tools and data sources. It defined how a server could expose capabilities, how clients could discover resources and prompts, and how structured tool invocation worked. It enabled early interoperability in agent ecosystems without requiring every integration to be a custom API.
However, the June version had notable limitations that restricted its use in production systems:
Execution was primarily synchronous. Long-running or complex work was not supported.
Authorization was basic, with a coarse trust model and limited granularity.
Client registration and identity handling were under-specified and inconsistent.
Governance and lifecycle controls were minimal.

specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/changelog) represents a significant shift. It expands MCP beyond synchronous tool calling into an architecture capable of supporting secure, long-running, governed workflows inside real production environments. excerpts: article demonstrates how to implement this robust, scalable pattern in **Google’s Agent Development Kit (ADK)** , enabling your agents to access thousands of tools (including MCP tools) with negligible latency
> How to reduce tool context consumption by 94% using Google’s Agent Development Kit (ADK) combined with a custom tool search.
The “Tool Search” pattern empowers AI agents to effectively manage hundreds or even thousands of tools by dynamically discovering and loading them strictly on demand.
The core concept is to split tool usage into two phases:

**Discovery
** The agent has access to a lightweight search tool. It queries a local index (using BM25) to find relevant tools based on names and docstrings

**Loading**
Once the agent identifies a useful tool, it calls a load tool. The system intercepts this call and dynamically injects the full tool definition into the agent's context for the next turn.

enabling your agents to access thousands of tools (including MCP tools) with negligible latency.

citations.title: Introducing advanced tool use on the Claude Developer Platform

excerpts: Programmatic tool calling allows Claude to write code that calls your tools programmatically within a code execution container, rather than requiring round trips through the model for each tool invocation.
This reduces latency for multi-tool workflows and decreases token consumption by allowing Claude to filter or process data before it reaches the model's context window.

agentic search benchmarks like [BrowseComp](https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.12516) and [DeepSearchQA](https://github.com/google-deepmind/deepsearchqa) , which test multi-step web research and complex information retrieval, adding programmatic tool calling on top of basic search tools was the key factor that fully unlocked agent performance.

The difference compounds fast in real workflows. Consider checking budget compliance across 20 employees: the traditional approach requires 20 separate model round-trips, pulling thousands of expense line items into the context along the way. With programmatic tool calling, a single script runs all 20 lookups, filters the results, and returns only the employees who exceeded their limits, shrinking what Claude needs to reason over from hundreds of kilobytes down to a handful of lines.
This feature requires the code execution tool to be enabled.
**Reason naturally about tool composition** : Chain operations and handle dependencies as naturally as writing any Python code
**Process large results efficiently** : Filter down large tool outputs, extract only relevant data, or write intermediate results to files before returning summaries to the context window
**Reduce latency significantly** : Eliminate the overhead of re-sampling Claude between each tool call in multi-step workflows

excerpts: September 8, 2025 · David Soria Parra (Lead Maintainer), Adam Jones (Registry Maintainer), Tadas Antanavicius (Registry Maintainer), Toby Padilla (Registry Maintainer), Theodora Chu (MCP PM at Anthropic)
Today, we’re launching the Model Context Protocol (MCP) Registry—an open catalog and API for publicly available MCP servers to improve discoverability and implementation.
Our goal is to standardize how servers are distributed and discovered, providing a primary source of truth that sub-registries can build upon.
The MCP Registry is now available in preview.
By standardizing how servers are distributed and discovered, we’re expanding their reach while making it easier for clients to get connected.

excerpts: Since launching MCP in November 2024, adoption has been rapid: the community has built thousands of [MCP servers](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/servers) , [SDKs](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/sdk) are available for all major programming languages, and the industry has adopted MCP as the de-facto standard for connecting agents to tools and data.

Tool descriptions occupy more context window space, increasing response time and costs. In cases where agents are connected to thousands of tools, they’ll need to process hundreds of thousands of tokens before reading a request.

Every intermediate result must pass through the model. In this example, the full call transcript flows through twice. For a 2-hour sales meeting, that could mean processing an additional 50,000 tokens. Even larger documents may exceed context window limits, breaking the workflow.
Models are great at navigating filesystems. Presenting tools as code on a filesystem allows models to read tool definitions on-demand, rather than reading them all up-front.
Alternatively, a `search_tools` tool can be added to the server to find relevant definitions.

Including a detail level parameter in the `search_tools` tool that allows the agent to select the level of detail required (such as name only, name and description, or the full definition with schemas) also helps the agent conserve context and find tools efficiently.
This reduces the token usage from 150,000 tokens to 2,000 tokens—a time and cost saving of 98.7% **.
With code execution environments becoming more common for agents, a solution is to present MCP servers as code APIs rather than direct tool calls.

excerpts:
The [June 2025 specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/changelog) established the first stable model of MCP for connecting agents to external tools and data sources. It defined how a server could expose capabilities, how clients could discover resources and prompts, and how structured tool invocation worked. It enabled early interoperability in agent ecosystems without requiring every integration to be a custom API.
However, the June version had notable limitations that restricted its use in production systems:
Execution was primarily synchronous. Long-running or complex work was not supported.
Authorization was basic, with a coarse trust model and limited granularity.
Client registration and identity handling were under-specified and inconsistent.
Governance and lifecycle controls were minimal.

specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/changelog) represents a significant shift. It expands MCP beyond synchronous tool calling into an architecture capable of supporting secure, long-running, governed workflows inside real production environments. It brings modern authorization, asynchronous execution, and enterprise considerations that begin to form the foundation of safe aut
The changes do not solve every challenge. Issues remain around registration consistency, workload identity, supply-chain provenance, and governance standards.

excerpts: Programmatic tool calling allows Claude to write code that calls your tools programmatically within a code execution container, rather than requiring round trips through the model for each tool invocation.
**Reason naturally about tool composition** : Chain operations and handle dependencies as naturally as writing any Python code
**Process large results efficiently** : Filter down large tool outputs, extract only relevant data, or write intermediate results to files before returning summaries to the context window
**Reduce latency significantly** : Eliminate the overhead of re-sampling Claude between each tool call in multi-step workflows

excerpts: When you build an MCP client and call `list_tools()` on an MCP server, you'll receive tool definitions with an `inputSchema` field. To use these tools with Claude, convert them to Claude's format:

from  mcp  import  ClientSession async  def  get_claude_tools ( mcp_session : ClientSession):     """Convert MCP tools to Claude's tool format."""     mcp_tools  =  await  mcp_session.list_tools()     claude_tools  =  []     for  tool  in  mcp_tools.tools:         claude_tools.append(             {                 "name" : tool.name,                 "description" : tool.description  or  "" ,                 "input_schema" : tool.inputSchema,   # Rename inputSchema to input_schema             }         )     return  claude_tools

excerpts: Programmatic tool calling allows Claude to write code that calls your tools programmatically within a code execution container, rather than requiring round trips through the model for each tool invocation.
This reduces latency for multi-tool workflows and decreases token consumption by allowing Claude to filter or process data before it reaches the model's context window.

agentic search benchmarks like [BrowseComp](https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.12516) and [DeepSearchQA](https://github.com/google-deepmind/deepsearchqa) , which test multi-step web research and complex information retrieval, adding programmatic tool calling on top of basic search tools was the key factor that fully unlocked agent performance.

The difference compounds fast in real workflows. Consider checking budget compliance across 20 employees: the traditional approach requires 20 separate model round-trips, pulling thousands of expense line items into the context along the way. With programmatic tool calling, a single script runs all 20 lookups, filters the results, and returns only the employees who exceeded their limits, shrinking what Claude needs to reason over from hundreds of kilobytes down to a handful of lines.
This feature requires the code execution tool to be enabled.
**Reason naturally about tool composition** : Chain operations and handle dependencies as naturally as writing any Python code
**Process large results efficiently** : Filter down large tool outputs, extract only relevant data, or write intermediate results to files before returning summaries to the context window
**Reduce latency significantly** : Eliminate the overhead of re-sampling Claude between each tool call in multi-step workflows

excerpts: Since launching MCP in November 2024, adoption has been rapid: the community has built thousands of [MCP servers](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/servers) , [SDKs](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/sdk) are available for all major programming languages, and the industry has adopted MCP as the de-facto standard for connecting agents to tools and data.

Tool descriptions occupy more context window space, increasing response time and costs. In cases where agents are connected to thousands of tools, they’ll need to process hundreds of thousands of tokens before reading a request.

Every intermediate result must pass through the model. In this example, the full call transcript flows through twice. For a 2-hour sales meeting, that could mean processing an additional 50,000 tokens. Even larger documents may exceed context window limits, breaking the workflow.
Models are great at navigating filesystems. Presenting tools as code on a filesystem allows models to read tool definitions on-demand, rather than reading them all up-front.
Alternatively, a `search_tools` tool can be added to the server to find relevant definitions.

Including a detail level parameter in the `search_tools` tool that allows the agent to select the level of detail required (such as name only, name and description, or the full definition with schemas) also helps the agent conserve context and find tools efficiently.
This reduces the token usage from 150,000 tokens to 2,000 tokens—a time and cost saving of 98.7% **.
With code execution environments becoming more common for agents, a solution is to present MCP servers as code APIs rather than direct tool calls.

excerpts: > How to reduce tool context consumption by 94% using Google’s Agent Development Kit (ADK) combined with a custom tool search.
The “Tool Search” pattern empowers AI agents to effectively manage hundreds or even thousands of tools by dynamically discovering and loading them strictly on demand.
Instead of overwhelming the model by dumping every possible tool definition into the context window upfront, this approach keeps the context small while still covering a large number of tools.
The core concept is to split tool usage into two phases:

**Discovery
** The agent has access to a lightweight search tool. It queries a local index (using BM25) to find relevant tools based on names and docstrings

**Loading**

The [June 2025 specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/changelog) established the first stable model of MCP for connecting agents to external tools and data sources. It defined how a server could expose capabilities, how clients could discover resources and prompts, and how structured tool invocation worked. It enabled early interoperability in agent ecosystems without requiring every integration to be a custom API.
However, the June version had notable limitations that restricted its use in production systems:
Execution was primarily synchronous. Long-running or complex work was not supported.
Authorization was basic, with a coarse trust model and limited granularity.
Client registration and identity handling were under-specified and inconsistent.
Governance and lifecycle controls were minimal.

excerpts: The “Tool Search” pattern empowers AI agents to effectively manage hundreds or even thousands of tools by dynamically discovering and loading them strictly on demand.
> How to reduce tool context consumption by 94% using Google’s Agent Development Kit (ADK) combined with a custom tool search.
Instead of overwhelming the model by dumping every possible tool definition into the context window upfront, this approach keeps the context small while still covering a large number of tools.
The core concept is to split tool usage into two phases:

**Discovery
** The agent has access to a lightweight search tool. It queries a local index (using BM25) to find relevant tools based on names and docstrings

**Loading**
Once the agent identifies a useful tool, it calls a load tool. The system intercepts this call and dynamically injects the full tool definition into the agent's context for the next turn.

article demonstrates how to implement this robust, scalable pattern in **Google’s Agent Development Kit (ADK)** , enabling your agents to access thousands of tools (including MCP tools) with negligible latency

enabling your agents to access thousands of tools (including MCP tools) with negligible latency.

excerpts: Programmatic tool calling allows Claude to write code that calls your tools programmatically within a code execution container, rather than requiring round trips through the model for each tool invocation.

agentic search benchmarks like [BrowseComp](https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.12516) and [DeepSearchQA](https://github.com/google-deepmind/deepsearchqa) , which test multi-step web research and complex information retrieval, adding programmatic tool calling on top of basic search tools was the key factor that fully unlocked agent performance.

The difference compounds fast in real workflows. Consider checking budget compliance across 20 employees: the traditional approach requires 20 separate model round-trips, pulling thousands of expense line items into the context along the way. With programmatic tool calling, a single script runs all 20 lookups, filters the results, and returns only the employees who exceeded their limits, shrinking what Claude needs to reason over from hundreds of kilobytes down to a handful of lines.
This feature requires the code execution tool to be enabled.
**Reason naturally about tool composition** : Chain operations and handle dependencies as naturally as writing any Python code
**Process large results efficiently** : Filter down large tool outputs, extract only relevant data, or write intermediate results to files before returning summaries to the context window
**Reduce latency significantly** : Eliminate the overhead of re-sampling Claude between each tool call in multi-step workflows

excerpts:
The [June 2025 specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/changelog) established the first stable model of MCP for connecting agents to external tools and data sources. It defined how a server could expose capabilities, how clients could discover resources and prompts, and how structured tool invocation worked. It enabled early interoperability in agent ecosystems without requiring every integration to be a custom API.
However, the June version had notable limitations that restricted its use in production systems:
Execution was primarily synchronous. Long-running or complex work was not supported.
Authorization was basic, with a coarse trust model and limited granularity.
Client registration and identity handling were under-specified and inconsistent.
Governance and lifecycle controls were minimal.

specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/changelog) represents a significant shift. It expands MCP beyond synchronous tool calling into an architecture capable of supporting secure, long-running, governed workflows inside real production environments. It brings modern authorization, asynchronous execution, and enterprise considerations that begin to form the foundation of safe aut
The changes do not solve every challenge. Issues remain around registration consistency, workload identity, supply-chain provenance, and governance standards.

excerpts:
The [June 2025 specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/changelog) established the first stable model of MCP for connecting agents to external tools and data sources. It defined how a server could expose capabilities, how clients could discover resources and prompts, and how structured tool invocation worked. It enabled early interoperability in agent ecosystems without requiring every integration to be a custom API.
However, the June version had notable limitations that restricted its use in production systems:
Execution was primarily synchronous. Long-running or complex work was not supported.
Authorization was basic, with a coarse trust model and limited granularity.
Client registration and identity handling were under-specified and inconsistent.
Governance and lifecycle controls were minimal.
The changes do not solve every challenge. Issues remain around registration consistency, workload identity, supply-chain provenance, and governance standards.

Enterprises are discovering the hard way that the challenge with AI agents is not generating answers, but governing actions. When an agent can push changes, orchestrate cloud services, or kick off long-running workflows, the conversation shifts from experimentation to accountability and operational trust.

enabling your agents to access thousands of tools (including MCP tools) with negligible latency.
The “Tool Search” pattern empowers AI agents to effectively manage hundreds or even thousands of tools by dynamically discovering and loading them strictly on demand.
The core concept is to split tool usage into two phases:

**Discovery
** The agent has access to a lightweight search tool. It queries a local index (using BM25) to find relevant tools based on names and docstrings

**Loading**
Once the agent identifies a useful tool, it calls a load tool. The system intercepts this call and dynamically injects the full tool definition into the agent's context for the next turn.
Anthropic recently introduced the Tool Search pattern:

[## Tool search tool ### Claude API Documentation platform.claude.com](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/tool-search-tool?source=post_page-----f39d02a35139---------------------------------------)

excerpts: If you're building an application that uses the [Model Context Protocol (MCP)](https://modelcontextprotocol.io) , you can use tools from MCP servers directly with Claude's Messages API. MCP tool definitions use a schema format that's similar to Claude's tool format. You just need to rename `inputSchema` to `input_schema` .
When you build an MCP client and call `list_tools()` on an MCP server, you'll receive tool definitions with an `inputSchema` field. To use these tools with Claude, convert them to Claude's format:

from  mcp  import  ClientSession async  def  get_claude_tools ( mcp_session : ClientSession):     """Convert MCP tools to Claude's tool format."""     mcp_tools  =  await  mcp_session.list_tools()     claude_tools  =  []     for  tool  in  mcp_tools.tools:         claude_tools.append(             {                 "name" : tool.name,                 "description" : tool.description  or  "" ,                 "input_schema" : tool.inputSchema,   # Rename inputSchema to input_schema             }         )     return  claude_tools

excerpts: Since launching MCP in November 2024, adoption has been rapid: the community has built thousands of [MCP servers](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/servers) , [SDKs](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/sdk) are available for all major programming languages, and the industry has adopted MCP as the de-facto standard for connecting agents to tools and data.

Tool descriptions occupy more context window space, increasing response time and costs. In cases where agents are connected to thousands of tools, they’ll need to process hundreds of thousands of tokens before reading a request.

Every intermediate result must pass through the model. In this example, the full call transcript flows through twice. For a 2-hour sales meeting, that could mean processing an additional 50,000 tokens. Even larger documents may exceed context window limits, breaking the workflow.
Models are great at navigating filesystems. Presenting tools as code on a filesystem allows models to read tool definitions on-demand, rather than reading them all up-front.
Alternatively, a `search_tools` tool can be added to the server to find relevant definitions.

Including a detail level parameter in the `search_tools` tool that allows the agent to select the level of detail required (such as name only, name and description, or the full definition with schemas) also helps the agent conserve context and find tools efficiently.
This reduces the token usage from 150,000 tokens to 2,000 tokens—a time and cost saving of 98.7% **.
With code execution environments becoming more common for agents, a solution is to present MCP servers as code APIs rather than direct tool calls.
citations.title: Code execution with MCP: building more efficient AI agents

The [June 2025 specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/changelog) established the first stable model of MCP for connecting agents to external tools and data sources. It defined how a server could expose capabilities, how clients could discover resources and prompts, and how structured tool invocation worked. It enabled early interoperability in agent ecosystems without requiring every integration to be a custom API.
However, the June version had notable limitations that restricted its use in production systems:
Execution was primarily synchronous. Long-running or complex work was not supported.
Authorization was basic, with a coarse trust model and limited granularity.
Client registration and identity handling were under-specified and inconsistent.
Governance and lifecycle controls were minimal.
The changes do not solve every challenge. Issues remain around registration consistency, workload identity, supply-chain provenance, and governance standards.

The MCP Registry is now available in preview.
By standardizing how servers are distributed and discovered, we’re expanding their reach while making it easier for clients to get connected.

excerpts: If you're building an application that uses the [Model Context Protocol (MCP)](https://modelcontextprotocol.io) , you can use tools from MCP servers directly with Claude's Messages API. MCP tool definitions use a schema format that's similar to Claude's tool format. You just need to rename `inputSchema` to `input_schema` .
When you build an MCP client and call `list_tools()` on an MCP server, you'll receive tool definitions with an `inputSchema` field. To use these tools with Claude, convert them to Claude's format:

from  mcp  import  ClientSession async  def  get_claude_tools ( mcp_session : ClientSession):     """Convert MCP tools to Claude's tool format."""     mcp_tools  =  await  mcp_session.list_tools()     claude_tools  =  []     for  tool  in  mcp_tools.tools:         claude_tools.append(             {                 "name" : tool.name,                 "description" : tool.description  or  "" ,                 "input_schema" : tool.inputSchema,   # Rename inputSchema to input_schema             }         )     return  claude_tools

from  mcp  import  ClientSession async  def  get_claude_tools ( mcp_session : ClientSession):     """Convert MCP tools to Claude's tool format."""     mcp_tools  =  await  mcp_session.list_tools()     claude_tools  =  []     for  tool  in  mcp_tools.tools:         claude_tools.append(             {                 "name" : tool.name,                 "description" : tool.description  or  "" ,                 "input_schema" : tool.inputSchema,   # Rename inputSchema to input_schema             }         )     return  claude_tools

excerpts: The “Tool Search” pattern empowers AI agents to effectively manage hundreds or even thousands of tools by dynamically discovering and loading them strictly on demand.
> How to reduce tool context consumption by 94% using Google’s Agent Development Kit (ADK) combined with a custom tool search.
Instead of overwhelming the model by dumping every possible tool definition into the context window upfront, this approach keeps the context small while still covering a large number of tools.
The core concept is to split tool usage into two phases:

**Discovery
** The agent has access to a lightweight search tool. It queries a local index (using BM25) to find relevant tools based on names and docstrings

**Loading**
Once the agent identifies a useful tool, it calls a load tool. The system intercepts this call and dynamically injects the full tool definition into the agent's context for the next turn.

article demonstrates how to implement this robust, scalable pattern in **Google’s Agent Development Kit (ADK)** , enabling your agents to access thousands of tools (including MCP tools) with negligible latency
Anthropic recently introduced the Tool Search pattern:

enabling your agents to access thousands of tools (including MCP tools) with negligible latency.

excerpts: Programmatic tool calling allows Claude to write code that calls your tools programmatically within a code execution container, rather than requiring round trips through the model for each tool invocation.

agentic search benchmarks like [BrowseComp](https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.12516) and [DeepSearchQA](https://github.com/google-deepmind/deepsearchqa) , which test multi-step web research and complex information retrieval, adding programmatic tool calling on top of basic search tools was the key factor that fully unlocked agent performance.

The difference compounds fast in real workflows. Consider checking budget compliance across 20 employees: the traditional approach requires 20 separate model round-trips, pulling thousands of expense line items into the context along the way. With programmatic tool calling, a single script runs all 20 lookups, filters the results, and returns only the employees who exceeded their limits, shrinking what Claude needs to reason over from hundreds of kilobytes down to a handful of lines.
This feature requires the code execution tool to be enabled.
**Reason naturally about tool composition** : Chain operations and handle dependencies as naturally as writing any Python code
**Process large results efficiently** : Filter down large tool outputs, extract only relevant data, or write intermediate results to files before returning summaries to the context window
**Reduce latency significantly** : Eliminate the overhead of re-sampling Claude between each tool call in multi-step workflows

excerpts: specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/changelog) represents a significant shift. It expands MCP beyond synchronous tool calling into an architecture capable of supporting secure, long-running, governed workflows inside real production environments. It brings modern authorization, asynchronous execution, and enterprise considerations that begin to form the foundation of safe aut

The [June 2025 specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/changelog) established the first stable model of MCP for connecting agents to external tools and data sources. It defined how a server could expose capabilities, how clients could discover resources and prompts, and how structured tool invocation worked. It enabled early interoperability in agent ecosystems without requiring every integration to be a custom API.
However, the June version had notable limitations that restricted its use in production systems:
Execution was primarily synchronous. Long-running or complex work was not supported.
Authorization was basic, with a coarse trust model and limited granularity.
Client registration and identity handling were under-specified and inconsistent.
Governance and lifecycle controls were minimal.

Enterprises are discovering the hard way that the challenge with AI agents is not generating answers, but governing actions. When an agent can push changes, orchestrate cloud services, or kick off long-running workflows, the conversation shifts from experimentation to accountability and operational trust.
The changes do not solve every challenge. Issues remain around registration consistency, workload identity, supply-chain provenance, and governance standards.

excerpts: **Reduce latency significantly** : Eliminate the overhead of re-sampling Claude between each tool call in multi-step workflows
This reduces latency for multi-tool workflows and decreases token consumption by allowing Claude to filter or process data before it reaches the model's context window.

The difference compounds fast in real workflows. Consider checking budget compliance across 20 employees: the traditional approach requires 20 separate model round-trips, pulling thousands of expense line items into the context along the way. With programmatic tool calling, a single script runs all 20 lookups, filters the results, and returns only the employees who exceeded their limits, shrinking what Claude needs to reason over from hundreds of kilobytes down to a handful of lines.

Programmatic tool calling allows Claude to write code that calls your tools programmatically within a code execution container, rather than requiring round trips through the model for each tool invocation.

agentic search benchmarks like [BrowseComp](https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.12516) and [DeepSearchQA](https://github.com/google-deepmind/deepsearchqa) , which test multi-step web research and complex information retrieval, adding programmatic tool calling on top of basic search tools was the key factor that fully unlocked agent performance.
This feature requires the code execution tool to be enabled.

enabling your agents to access thousands of tools (including MCP tools) with negligible latency.

**Loading**

basis.reasoning: The strongest support for a context-compaction technique is explicit evidence of reducing the contextual burden while maintaining tool availability: a reported context reduction of 94.54% alongside a saved context footprint, which directly embodies the idea of compaction. Supporting details describe practical mechanisms that enable context-efficient tool surfaces: dynamically injecting full tool definitions into the agent's context only when a tool is identified as useful (lazy loading), and using a lightweight discovery/search workflow to identify relevant tools without loading everything preemptively. Taken together, these excerpts illustrate concrete methods—context saving, on-demand loading, lightweight search, and established tool-surface patterns—that implement the concept of Context Compaction in tool management for LLM agents.

excerpts: Anthropic recently introduced the Tool Search pattern:
The “Tool Search” pattern empowers AI agents to effectively manage hundreds or even thousands of tools by dynamically discovering and loading them strictly on demand.

[## Tool search tool ### Claude API Documentation platform.claude.com](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/tool-search-tool?source=post_page-----f39d02a35139---------------------------------------)
> How to reduce tool context consumption by 94% using Google’s Agent Development Kit (ADK) combined with a custom tool search.

article demonstrates how to implement this robust, scalable pattern in **Google’s Agent Development Kit (ADK)** , enabling your agents to access thousands of tools (including MCP tools) with negligible latency
Instead of overwhelming the model by dumping every possible tool definition into the context window upfront, this approach keeps the context small while still covering a large number of tools.

**Discovery
** The agent has access to a lightweight search tool. It queries a local index (using BM25) to find relevant tools based on names and docstrings

**Loading**
Once the agent identifies a useful tool, it calls a load tool. The system intercepts this call and dynamically injects the full tool definition into the agent's context for the next turn.
enabling your agents to access thousands of tools (including MCP tools) with negligible latency.

excerpts:
The [June 2025 specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/changelog) established the first stable model of MCP for connecting agents to external tools and data sources. It defined how a server could expose capabilities, how clients could discover resources and prompts, and how structured tool invocation worked. It enabled early interoperability in agent ecosystems without requiring every integration to be a custom API.
However, the June version had notable limitations that restricted its use in production systems:
Execution was primarily synchronous. Long-running or complex work was not supported.
Authorization was basic, with a coarse trust model and limited granularity.
Client registration and identity handling were under-specified and inconsistent.
Governance and lifecycle controls were minimal.

excerpts:
The [June 2025 specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/changelog) established the first stable model of MCP for connecting agents to external tools and data sources. It defined how a server could expose capabilities, how clients could discover resources and prompts, and how structured tool invocation worked. It enabled early interoperability in agent ecosystems without requiring every integration to be a custom API.
However, the June version had notable limitations that restricted its use in production systems:
Execution was primarily synchronous. Long-running or complex work was not supported.
Authorization was basic, with a coarse trust model and limited granularity.
Client registration and identity handling were under-specified and inconsistent.
Governance and lifecycle controls were minimal.

specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/changelog) represents a significant shift. It expands MCP beyond synchronous tool calling into an architecture capable of supporting secure, long-running, governed workflows inside real production environments. It brings modern authorization, asynchronous execution, and enterprise considerations that begin to form the foundation of safe aut
The changes do not solve every challenge. Issues remain around registration consistency, workload identity, supply-chain provenance, and governance standards.

*MCP Registry** (a global directory of available servers) had grown to thousands of entries and was becoming an important part of the ecosystem for discovery of context providers .

Our goal is to standardize how servers are distributed and discovered, providing a primary source of truth that sub-registries can build upon.
The MCP Registry is now available in preview.
By standardizing how servers are distributed and discovered, we’re expanding their reach while making it easier for clients to get connected.

excerpts: The “Tool Search” pattern empowers AI agents to effectively manage hundreds or even thousands of tools by dynamically discovering and loading them strictly on demand.
Anthropic recently introduced the Tool Search pattern:

article demonstrates how to implement this robust, scalable pattern in **Google’s Agent Development Kit (ADK)** , enabling your agents to access thousands of tools (including MCP tools) with negligible latency

**Discovery
** The agent has access to a lightweight search tool. It queries a local index (using BM25) to find relevant tools based on names and docstrings

**Loading**
Once the agent identifies a useful tool, it calls a load tool. The system intercepts this call and dynamically injects the full tool definition into the agent's context for the next turn.
Instead of overwhelming the model by dumping every possible tool definition into the context window upfront, this approach keeps the context small while still covering a large number of tools.
> How to reduce tool context consumption by 94% using Google’s Agent Development Kit (ADK) combined with a custom tool search.
enabling your agents to access thousands of tools (including MCP tools) with negligible latency.

excerpts: Across the ecosystem: There are now more than 10,000 active public MCP servers, covering everything from developer tools to Fortune 500 deployments;

excerpts:
The [June 2025 specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/changelog) established the first stable model of MCP for connecting agents to external tools and data sources. It defined how a server could expose capabilities, how clients could discover resources and prompts, and how structured tool invocation worked. It enabled early interoperability in agent ecosystems without requiring every integration to be a custom API.
However, the June version had notable limitations that restricted its use in production systems:
Execution was primarily synchronous. Long-running or complex work was not supported.
Authorization was basic, with a coarse trust model and limited granularity.
Client registration and identity handling were under-specified and inconsistent.
Governance and lifecycle controls were minimal.

specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/changelog) represents a significant shift. It expands MCP beyond synchronous tool calling into an architecture capable of supporting secure, long-running, governed workflows inside real production environments. It brings modern authorization, asynchronous execution, and enterprise considerations that begin to form the foundation of safe aut
The changes do not solve every challenge. Issues remain around registration consistency, workload identity, supply-chain provenance, and governance standards.

The [June 2025 specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/changelog) established the first stable model of MCP for connecting agents to external tools and data sources. It defined how a server could expose capabilities, how clients could discover resources and prompts, and how structured tool invocation worked. It enabled early interoperability in agent ecosystems without requiring every integration to be a custom API.
However, the June version had notable limitations that restricted its use in production systems:
Execution was primarily synchronous. Long-running or complex work was not supported.
Authorization was basic, with a coarse trust model and limited granularity.
Client registration and identity handling were under-specified and inconsistent.
Governance and lifecycle controls were minimal.
The changes do not solve every challenge. Issues remain around registration consistency, workload identity, supply-chain provenance, and governance standards.

The [June 2025 specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/changelog) established the first stable model of MCP for connecting agents to external tools and data sources. It defined how a server could expose capabilities, how clients could discover resources and prompts, and how structured tool invocation worked. It enabled early interoperability in agent ecosystems without requiring every integration to be a custom API.
However, the June version had notable limitations that restricted its use in production systems:
Execution was primarily synchronous. Long-running or complex work was not supported.
Authorization was basic, with a coarse trust model and limited granularity.
Client registration and identity handling were under-specified and inconsistent.
Governance and lifecycle controls were minimal.

specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/changelog) represents a significant shift. It expands MCP beyond synchronous tool calling into an architecture capable of supporting secure, long-running, governed workflows inside real production environments. It brings modern authorization, asynchronous execution, and enterprise considerations that begin to form the foundation of safe aut
The changes do not solve every challenge. Issues remain around registration consistency, workload identity, supply-chain provenance, and governance standards.
citations.title: MCP’s Next Phase: Inside the November 2025 Specification | by Dave Patten | Medium

excerpts: Today, we’re launching the Model Context Protocol (MCP) Registry—an open catalog and API for publicly available MCP servers to improve discoverability and implementation.
Our goal is to standardize how servers are distributed and discovered, providing a primary source of truth that sub-registries can build upon.
The MCP Registry is now available in preview.
By standardizing how servers are distributed and discovered, we’re expanding their reach while making it easier for clients to get connected.

excerpts: Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open protocol that enables seamless integration between LLM applications and external data sources and tools.
citations.title: Specification - What is the Model Context Protocol (MCP)?

excerpts: MCP now has an official, community-driven [Registry](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/registry) for discovering available MCP servers, and the [November 25th](https://blog.modelcontextprotocol.io/posts/2025-11-25-first-mcp-anniversary/) spec release introduced many new features, including asynchronous operations, statelessness, server identity, and official extensions.

Claude now has a directory with over 75 [connectors](https://claude.com/connectors) (powered by MCP), and we recently launched [Tool Search and Programmatic Tool Calling](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/advanced-tool-use) capabilities in our API to help optimize production-scale MCP deployments, handling thousands of tools efficiently and reducing latency in complex agent workflows.
Across the ecosystem: There are now more than 10,000 active public MCP servers, covering everything from developer tools to Fortune 500 deployments;

Dec 9, 2025
Today, we’re donating the [Model Context Protocol](https://modelcontextprotocol.io) (MCP) to the Agentic AI Foundation (AAIF), a directed fund under the [Linux Foundation](https://www.linuxfoundation.org/) , co-founded by Anthropic, Block and OpenAI, with support from Google, Microsoft, Amazon Web Services (AWS), Cloudflare, and Bloomberg.

excerpts: article demonstrates how to implement this robust, scalable pattern in **Google’s Agent Development Kit (ADK)** , enabling your agents to access thousands of tools (including MCP tools) with negligible latency
The “Tool Search” pattern empowers AI agents to effectively manage hundreds or even thousands of tools by dynamically discovering and loading them strictly on demand.

**Discovery
** The agent has access to a lightweight search tool. It queries a local index (using BM25) to find relevant tools based on names and docstrings

**Loading**
Once the agent identifies a useful tool, it calls a load tool. The system intercepts this call and dynamically injects the full tool definition into the agent's context for the next turn.
enabling your agents to access thousands of tools (including MCP tools) with negligible latency.
Anthropic recently introduced the Tool Search pattern:
The core concept is to split tool usage into two phases:

excerpts: Our recently launched Agent2Agent (A2A) protocol is getting a lot of attention, and for good reason. This open interoperability protocol is designed to make it easy for AI agents to communicate with one another, no matter its foundation. It’s also a powerful complement to Anthropic’s Model Context Protocol (MCP).
The protocol can be used as an extension of the Agent2Agent (A2A) protocol and Model Context Protocol (MCP).

Once the agent identifies a useful tool, it calls a load tool. The system intercepts this call and dynamically injects the full tool definition into the agent's context for the next turn.

It expands MCP beyond synchronous tool calling into an architecture capable of supporting secure, long-running, governed workflows inside real production environments. It brings modern authorization, asynchronous execution, and enterprise considerations that begin to form the foundation of safe aut

The [June 2025 specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/changelog) established the first stable model of MCP for connecting agents to external tools and data sources. It defined how a server could expose capabilities, how clients could discover resources and prompts, and how structured tool invocation worked. It enabled early interoperability in agent ecosystems without requiring every integration to be a custom API.
However, the June version had notable limitations that restricted its use in production systems:
Execution was primarily synchronous. Long-running or complex work was not supported.
Authorization was basic, with a coarse trust model and limited granularity.
Client registration and identity handling were under-specified and inconsistent.
Governance and lifecycle controls were minimal.
The changes do not solve every challenge. Issues remain around registration consistency, workload identity, supply-chain provenance, and governance standards.

executive_summary.description: A high-level overview of the major trends and shifts in agentic AI tool surface design and protocol evolution between 2025 and 2026. This summary addresses the convergence on standards, new architectural patterns, and the key challenges being tackled.

development.description: The name or title of the key development.

description.description: A brief description of the development and its significance.

category.description: The category of the development (e.g., Protocol Standardization, Platform API, Architectural Pattern).

impact.description: The impact of this development on the agentic AI ecosystem.

key_developments_overview.description: A list of the most significant developments in the agentic AI landscape for 2025-2026, including the standardization of protocols, major platform API updates, and new techniques for managing complex tool interactions.

update_date.description: The date of the specification update or milestone (e.g., 'March 26, 2025').

version_or_milestone.description: The version number or name of the milestone (e.g., 'MCP v2', 'Anniversary Release').

key_features_introduced.description: A summary of the major features introduced in this update, such as 'Streamable HTTP transport', 'OAuth 2.1 framework', or 'Tasks API for async operations'.

significance.description: The overall significance of this update (e.g., 'Made MCP cloud-ready', 'Enabled long-running workflows').

mcp_evolution_timeline.description: A chronological account of the Model Context Protocol (MCP) evolution through 2025-2026. Details include key specification updates (e.g., March, June, November 2025), and features introduced such as Streamable HTTP transport, OAuth 2.1 security, structured tool outputs, elicitation, and the Tasks API for asynchronous operations.

adoption_status.description: The status of MCP adoption in the industry (e.g., 'de-facto standard').

governing_body.description: The foundation or organization governing the protocol, such as 'Agentic AI Foundation (AAIF) under the Linux Foundation'.

active_servers_count.description: The approximate number of active public MCP servers.

key_adopters.description: A list of major platforms and products that have adopted MCP (e.g., 'ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Copilot').

protocol_name.description: The name of the protocol, 'Agent-to-Agent (A2A) Protocol'.

developer.description: The primary developer of the protocol (e.g., 'Google').

primary_purpose.description: The main goal of the protocol, such as 'Enabling communication and collaboration between agents from different vendors'.

key_feature.description: A standout feature of the protocol, like 'Agent Cards for discovery and capability negotiation'.

relationship_with_mcp.description: How A2A relates to MCP (e.g., 'Complementary: MCP connects agents to resources, A2A connects agents to each other').

api_or_feature.description: The name of the API or feature, such as 'Responses API' or 'Connectors'.

description.description: A description of the feature and its purpose, highlighting the shift towards more agentic behavior.

key_capabilities.description: Specific capabilities offered, such as 'Stateful context', 'Built-in tools (web_search, code_interpreter)', 'Remote MCP server support'.

release_period.description: The time frame when this evolution primarily occurred (e.g., '2025').

openai_tool_use_evolution.description: A detailed breakdown of OpenAI's approach to tool use, focusing on the evolution from the Chat Completions API to the more agentic 'Responses API'. Includes information on new models (GPT-5, o-series), stateful context management, built-in tools (web search, code interpreter), and support for remote MCP servers.

feature_name.description: The specific name of the advanced tool use feature (e.g., 'Tool Search Tool', 'Programmatic Tool Calling').

problem_solved.description: The primary challenge this feature addresses, such as 'Context bloat from large tool libraries' or 'High latency from multiple API round-trips'.

mechanism.description: How the feature works, for example 'Dynamically discovers and loads tools on-demand' or 'Allows Claude to write and execute code to orchestrate tools'.

api_implementation_detail.description: A key detail about its implementation, such as the 'defer_loading: true' parameter for the Tool Search Tool.

anthropic_claude_tool_use_evolution.description: An in-depth look at Anthropic's advanced features for managing large tool surfaces with Claude. This covers the 'Tool Search Tool' for dynamic discovery and lazy loading, 'Programmatic Tool Calling' for code-based orchestration, and 'Tool Use Examples' for improving invocation accuracy.

component_name.description: The name of the tool or framework in Google's ecosystem (e.g., 'Agent Development Kit (ADK)', 'Agent Engine').

component_type.description: The type of component (e.g., 'Open-source SDK', 'Fully managed runtime').

purpose.description: The primary purpose of the component, such as 'For designing agents' or 'For deploying custom agents to production at scale'.

google_vertex_ai_tool_approach.description: An overview of Google's ecosystem for building and deploying agents. Includes details on the Agent Development Kit (ADK), the fully managed Agent Engine runtime, Gemini's function calling capabilities, and the use of Vertex AI Extensions.

platform.description: The name of the major platform (e.g., 'Microsoft', 'AWS').

framework_or_service.description: The name of the specific agent framework or service offered (e.g., 'Microsoft Agent Framework', 'Bedrock Agents').

key_characteristic.description: A defining characteristic, such as 'Unified AutoGen and Semantic Kernel' or 'Includes AgentCore Policy service for deterministic enforcement'.

other_major_platform_approaches.description: A summary of tool and agent frameworks from other major cloud providers, including Microsoft's unified Microsoft Agent Framework (combining AutoGen and Semantic Kernel) and AWS Bedrock Agents with its AgentCore Policy service.

strategies_for_managing_large_tool_surfa.description: Analysis of the primary challenge of 'context bloat' from large tool libraries (100+ tools) and the key strategies developed to mitigate it. This synthesizes the shift from upfront loading to on-demand discovery and efficient code-based execution.

technique.description: The name of the technique (e.g., 'Tool Search', 'Lazy Loading', 'Programmatic Calling').

description.description: How the technique works to manage large tool surfaces.

primary_benefit.description: The main advantage of using this technique, such as 'Reduces initial context size' or 'Lowers latency in multi-step workflows'.

example_implementation.description: A specific example from the research, like 'Anthropic's `defer_loading: true`' or 'Presenting tools as a file system for progressive disclosure'.

dynamic_tool_management_techniques.description: A list of specific techniques used in production systems for managing large tool catalogs. Includes tool search (using regex, BM25, or embeddings), lazy loading (e.g., Anthropic's 'defer_loading'), tool catalog compression, and presenting tools as a file system for progressive disclosure.

tool_composition_and_validation_patterns.description: Description of new patterns for tool composition, chaining, and validation. Covers the use of tool graphs (e.g., LangGraph), schema-based validation (JSON Schema, Pydantic), and the implementation of guardrails for safety and policy enforcement.

tool_metadata_and_hints.description: Information on the use of metadata within tool schemas to provide hints to the LLM agent. This includes emerging but not yet standardized practices for indicating concurrency limits, execution cost, and timeout expectations.

registry_name.description: The official name of the registry or service (e.g., 'MCP Registry', 'Docker MCP Catalog').

purpose.description: The primary function of the registry, such as 'To provide a discoverable list of public MCP servers'.

sponsoring_organization.description: The organization that maintains or sponsors the registry (e.g., 'MCP open source project', 'Docker').

status.description: The current status of the registry (e.g., 'Live in preview', 'Generally available').

tool_registries_and_discovery_services.description: Details on centralized services for discovering and managing tools. Focuses on the official MCP Registry for finding public MCP servers and other initiatives like the Docker MCP Catalog.

emerging_architectural_patterns.description: An overview of high-level architectural patterns that have become prevalent in agentic systems, such as the definitive shift from linear chains to graph-based execution, the use of supervisor-worker patterns, and the establishment of memory as a first-class architectural primitive.

guideline_or_standard.description: A key industry standard or guideline, such as 'OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications'.

design_principle.description: An emerging design principle for safety, like 'Least Agency'.

infrastructure_solution.description: A type of infrastructure used for enforcement, such as 'MCP gateways' or 'Agent-native vaults (e.g., Peta)'.

key_risk_addressed.description: The type of risk being mitigated, such as 'Agent Goal Hijack', 'Rogue Agents', or 'Secret-in-hand problem'.

challenge_area.description: The specific area of the unresolved challenge (e.g., 'Unified Agent Discovery', 'Cross-Protocol State Management', 'Universal Agent Identity', 'Evaluation Infrastructure').

description.description: A brief explanation of the gap or problem that still needs to be solved.

current_status.description: The current state of this challenge (e.g., 'No standard exists', 'Nascent solutions emerging').
